summary: Nadia explores why software developers struggle to monetize their own code despite their skills being highly valued in the job market. She traces three eras of developer monetization: selling code (1980s-90s, largely failed due to zero marginal cost and developers creating alternatives), selling time (late 1990s-present, works but doesn't let developers profit from their own projects), and selling access/reputation (emerging). The piece argues that as code becomes increasingly commoditized and automatable, a developer's unique reputation and personal brand may become more valuable than the code itself. She suggests developers could charge for prioritization, knowledge sharing, or brand association rather than trying to sell code directly.
key_points: Developers' code-writing skills have market value, but their output (the code itself) often has none due to zero marginal cost and easy replication
Selling to developers is like 'selling ice to an Eskimo' because they can build alternatives themselves, especially for fun problems
Employment funds developers' lifestyles but forces them to write employer-chosen code rather than their own projects
As code becomes more interchangeable and automatable, fewer developers may be needed to perform the same tasks over time
A developer's reputation has fewer substitutes than their code, making it a potentially more sustainable funding mechanism
money_quotes: When a developer produces code of their own choosing, they quickly find that while their code-writing skills have market value, their output (the code they write) has none.
Selling to developers is a bit like selling ice to an Eskimo.
If code is your output, and code is basically knowledge, then over time, we don't need to write as much code as we used to.

So long as we focus on code, we'll keep coming back to the problem of substitutes. Code has many substitutes. Getting paid to write code has many substitutes (whether human or machine). But a developer's reputation has fewer substitutes.
The definition of open source says nothing about how it gets produced, only how it is distributed.
In the early 1990s, it seemed inconceivable that people wouldn't pay for software. Today, it might seem inconceivable that developers might eventually not sell their time.
key_insight: As code becomes increasingly commoditized and automatable, developers' sustainable path to capturing value may shift from selling code or time to monetizing their irreplaceable personal reputation and brand.
